query: chest wall and lung recoil balance during relaxation

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Functional residual capacity (FRC) is the volume remaining in the lungs after a normal, passive exhalation. In a normal individual, this is about 3L. The FRC also represents the point of the breathing cycle where the lung tissue elastic recoil and chest wall outward expansion are balanced and equal. Thus, the FRC is unique because it is both a volume and related directly to 2 respiratory structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pulmonary Compliance -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The lung and chest wall, together, form a combined compliance system. Independently, each lung and chest wall measures higher compliance than the combined lung-chest wall system. In addition to lung compliance, the combined system factors in the opposing force of the chest wall muscles and diaphragm. These muscles provide the necessary pressure difference for air movement. The combined lung-chest wall system is at equilibrium (no inclination toward collapse or expansion) when lung volume is at functional residual capacity (FRC), the remaining lung volume after tidal volume has expired. The 2 opposing forces of the chest and lungs set the negative intrapleural pressure. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pulmonary -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Chest wall compliance is similarly based on elastic properties. However, this is more of a balance of chest wall elastic recoil, which tries to increase lung volume, and the lung’s elastic properties, which are trying to decrease lung volume. Airway resistance is based on the physics principle of Ohm’s law where: Flow rate = change in pressure/resistance of the airway Resistance = 8(viscosity of air)(length of the tube)/(3.14)(radius of the tube)^4

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: To easily understand respiratory physiology, lungs can be compared to balloons. Balloons are elastic structures that tend to collapse if nothing is preventing the air from inside to leak out. On the other hand, the chest wall also has elastic recoil, which, at rest, tends to expand. The pleural space, filled with pleural fluid, is what juxtaposes the two surfaces. The pressure difference between the alveolar pressure and the intrapleural pressure is the transmural pressure across the lung (i.e., TPP). The pressure difference between the intrapleural pressure and the atmospheric pressure is the transmural pressure across the chest wall (CWP). At rest, the TPP is approximately 4 mmHg, and the CWP is approximately -4 mmHg. In other words, at rest, the inward elastic recoil of the lung exactly opposes the outward acting elastic recoil of the chest wall, and there is no net airflow. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: During passive expiration, the diaphragm and inspiratory intercostal muscles cease contracting and relax, resulting in inward recoil of the chest wall and a decrease in the lung size. The intrapleural pressure increases to its baseline value, which decreases the TPP. At this point, the TPP holding the lungs open is smaller than the elastic recoil exerted by the more inflated lungs, resulting in the passive recoil of the lungs to their baseline dimensions. Decreased lung dimensions lead to the alveolar pressure surpassing the atmospheric pressure, as explained by the Boyle law. As a result, air flows from the alveoli to the atmosphere until reaching a new equilibrium between the two pressures.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Residual Volume -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Residual volume can be understood by investigating breathing that exceeds tidal volumes. Following maximal inspiration, the volume of air that leaves the lungs during a maximal force expiration is the vital capacity (VC). VC comprises the tidal volume, expiratory reserve volume (ERV), and inspiratory reserve volume (IRV). The ERV is the volume of air that can be forcefully exhaled after a normal resting expiration, leaving only the RV in the lungs. Forcefully exhaling the ERV is an active process requiring the contraction of expiratory muscles in the chest and abdomen. This increases Ppl and Palv above atmospheric pressure. Due to the elastic recoil of the alveoli, the pressure inside of the alveoli remains higher than that of the pleura, and the alveoli remain open. The pressure inside the airways (Paw) slowly decreases as you move up from the alveoli to the trachea due to increased airway resistance. In sections of small, non-cartilaginous airways, pleural pressure is greater than airway pressure and causes a collapse of the airway (Figure 1B). The residual volume is the air that remains in the lungs after the collapse of all small airways.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tidal Volume -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, expiration is generally a passive process that occurs due to the lungs' elastic properties once the diaphragm relaxes. Relaxation of the diaphragm causes the rib cage to move closer to the lungs leading to an increase in intrapleural pressure back to -5 cm H2o. As a result, lung volume decreases, and pressure becomes higher than atmospheric pressure. This forces air out of the lungs as per pressure difference and the lungs get back to their resting state.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Residual Volume -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Although breathing mechanics are complex, it is important to remember that air flows from high-pressure to low-pressure areas. During tidal breathing, the inspiration and expiration at physiologic rest, the volume of air entering and leaving the lungs is known as the tidal volume (TV). On tidal inspiration, inspiratory muscle contraction increases the volume of the chest, causing the intrapleural pressure (Ppl) to drop from -5 cm H2O to -8 cm H2O. The decreased Ppl causes the alveolar pressure (Palv) to decrease 1 cm H2O below atmospheric pressure. As a result, air from the relatively high-pressure atmosphere flows into the low-pressure alveoli. Inspiration is an active process requiring the rhythmic contraction of inspiratory muscles that work to expand the chest cavity. Tidal expiration is a passive process that works in reverse. The inspiratory muscles relax, decreasing the size of the chest cavity and increasing Ppl and Palv. Once Palv is greater than atmospheric pressure, air flows out of the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: Postural Drainage and Vibration -- Technique or Treatment -- Active Cycle of Breathing Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Patients initially focus on controlled breathing to regulate their respiratory rate and promote relaxation. Thoracic expansion exercises are then performed, involving deep inhalation to expand the chest and increase lung volume. Patients then perform forced expiratory efforts and controlled coughing to effectively mobilize and clear mucus from the airways. Afterward, the relaxation phase allows patients to rest and recover, promoting overall comfort and well-being.

retrieved_docs.title: Atelectasis (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: There are four types of nonobstructive atelectasis: compression, adhesive, cicatrization, relaxation, and replacement atelectasis.  Compression atelectasis happens when there is increased pressure exerted on the lung which causes a  transmural pressure difference between the extra and intra-alveolar space that results in alveolar collapse. During anesthesia, diaphragmatic relaxation occurs inhibiting the natural lowering of the diaphragm that occurs during spontaneous breathing.     Lying in the supine position further displaces the diaphragm toward the head resulting in additional inhibition of gas exchange due to the further impairment of the transmural pressure gradient resulting in an increased risk of atelectasis.     Adhesive atelectasis occurs due to either a surfactant deficiency or dysfunction.  This type is commonly seen in patients with  Adult Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) or among premature infants with Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS). Surfactant prevents alveolar collapse by decreasing alveolar surface tension.  Alterations to surfactant production and function tend to increase the surface tension within the alveoli lead creating instability resulting in collapse.  Cicatrization atelectasis results in the contraction of the lung tissue to the development of parenchymal scar tissue.  Cicatrization atelectasis is often seen in tuberculosis, fibrosis, and other chronic destructive lung diseases. Relaxation atelectasis occurs when there is a loss of contact between the parietal and visceral tissue.  This happens with pneumothoraces and pleural effusions. Replacement atelectasis occurs when a tumor replaces the alveoli of an entire lobe of the lung, seen with bronchioalveolar carcinoma, resulting in the complete collapse of the lung.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Respiratory Rate -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Feedback from stretch receptors of muscles, tendons, and joints about the elastic recoil of the lung and the thoracic wall modifies the respiratory rate so that the lung optimizes the respiratory work, maximizes the gas exchange, and minimizes the mechanical breathing work. Such feedback impulses also get relayed by parenchymal lung receptors. The Hering-Breuer reflex prevents excessive distension of lung parenchyma; lung overinflation stimulates parenchymal stretch receptors via the vagus nerve, inhibits the respiratory center, and also prevents the apneustic center in the medulla from sending activating impulses to the respiratory center. The result of these processes is expiration. [6] Hyperinflation of the lung or the enlargement of lung capillaries starts nociceptors known as juxtacapillary receptors (J-receptors) that stimulate the respiratory center via the vagus nerve, increasing breathing rate. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Flail Chest -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Airflow into and out of the lungs depends on changes in intrathoracic pressure. Inspiration requires coordinated activation of respiratory muscle groups, including the diaphragm, external intercostals, parasternal muscles, internal intercostals, and accessory muscles. Descent of the diaphragmatic dome increases the vertical dimension of the thoracic cavity and generates negative pressure. At rest, the diaphragm alone can sustain adequate ventilation. During exercise or in pathological states, the intercostal muscles play a significant role in generating inspiratory effort. Exhalation is typically passive due to the elastic recoil of the lungs, although the abdominal and intercostal muscles may assist.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The FRC is affected by conditions that affect lung compliance, a combination of the lung's inward elastic recoil and the chest wall's outward expansion. These include diseases or conditions with changes in lung tissue compliance (emphysema and interstitial lung diseases), decreased chest movements (kyphoscoliosis), or decreased thoracic volume (obesity, pregnancy). Other factors affecting FRC include acute position changes such as lying supine, age, height, and gender.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function -- Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics alter FRC by affecting the tone or relaxation of the respiratory muscles. The contribution of the rib cage and diaphragm to decreased FRC is debated.

retrieved_docs.title: Atelectasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atelectasis, characterized by the partial or complete collapse of the lung or a section of the lung, is a common and significant clinical problem encountered in patients, particularly following surgery or in those with prolonged bed rest. This condition, resulting in impaired gas exchange and respiratory function, can lead to severe complications if not promptly addressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function -- Position
retrieved_docs.content: FRC is altered by the patients’ positioning, which is greatest when upright and decreases when supine or prone, resulting in airway closure of some lung regions. Even larger changes can be observed with patients in the Trendelenburg and head-down positions. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.

Finally, the clinical syndrome “دبيلة” is associated with the triad of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, linking these respiratory complications to a specific disease entity. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how precise anatomical localization—mediastinum, chest, and thoracic subdivisions—drives the classification, imaging, and intervention of lower‑respiratory and mediastinal pathologies, supporting accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1344
retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is linked to a specific Subdivision of thorax, indicating that this procedure is performed within a defined thoracic region. The Subdivision of thorax is encompassed by the mediastinum, the central compartment of the thoracic cavity that contains vital structures. Imaging of the mediastinum with thoracic CT scans can directly identify mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, which may be present bilaterally, on the right side, or on the left side. Recognizing these fistulas on thoracic CT scans of the mediastinum is essential for guiding the appropriate use of closed chest drainage and for planning further therapeutic interventions.

This subdivision of the thorax is directly linked to the thoracic conditions pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. The three pathologies—pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse—are also associated with the term “دبيلة”. Recognizing that closed drainage of chest is employed to treat pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse underscores its importance in thoracic management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum, a central subdivision of the thorax, serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower respiratory diseases such as lung and mediastinal abscesses (خراج) and is implicated in mediastinal disorders (اضطراب منصفي) including effusions. Mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية)—encompassing mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, and calcified variants—along with mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (unilateral right, unilateral left, and bilateral) are key thoracic pathologies. Chest computed tomography (CT), classified under imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract, is the primary diagnostic modality for visualizing these conditions. Standard chest CT, thoracic CT without contrast, and specialized thoracic CT scans (e.g., “thoracic CT scan: mediastinal‑pleural fistula”) function as parent procedures that generate specific findings such as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” and “mediastinal effusion.” The chest CT hierarchy also includes pulmonary angiographic imaging as a child study. Closed chest drainage is an interventional procedure linked to the thoracic subdivision and guided by chest CT imaging. Breast CT, while primarily a breast imaging study, is positioned inversely to radiographic breast imaging and is subsumed under the broader chest CT category, reflecting overlap between thoracic and breast imaging. Overall, the network emphasizes the mediastinum as a pivotal structure in lower respiratory disease, abscess formation, mass lesions, and fistulous complications, with chest CT—particularly non‑contrast thoracic protocols—serving as the essential tool for diagnosis and procedural planning.

retrieved_docs.content: Plication of emphysematous bleb is a specific surgical technique that falls within the broader category of بضع الصدر, a chest‑plication procedure used to reduce lung volume and treat conditions such as emphysema. This relationship indicates that بضع الصدر encompasses various methods—including the targeted plication of emphysematous blebs—to improve respiratory mechanics and patient outcomes.
